options: Hello World
Hello\nWorld
Compilation error
HelloWorld
explanation: `cout <<` outputs to standard output. The `<<` operator chains output. `endl` flushes the buffer and adds a newline but doesn't change what's printed on that line. Result: 'Hello World'.

options: cout
cin
scanf
read
explanation: `cin` is the standard input stream. The `>>` extraction operator reads from standard input into the variable. `cout` is for output, `scanf` is C-style (works but not idiomatic C++), and `read` is not standard.

question:
What is the difference between a `.h` file and a `.cpp` file in C++?

```
InventoryBin.h   → ???
InventoryBin.cpp → ???
```
options: .h contains the implementation; .cpp contains the declaration
.h contains class declarations (interface/headers); .cpp contains the actual method implementations
.h files are only for #include statements
There is no functional difference
explanation: In C++, .h (header) files declare the class structure — member variables, method signatures, and access specifiers. .cpp (source) files contain the actual method implementations. Other files include the .h to know what the class looks like.

options: GearBin implements the InventoryBin interface
GearBin publicly inherits from InventoryBin — it gets all of InventoryBin's public and protected members
GearBin is a private member of InventoryBin
GearBin and InventoryBin are the same class with different names
explanation: `: public InventoryBin` means GearBin inherits from InventoryBin with public access — public and protected members of InventoryBin remain accessible in GearBin. This is how inheritance works in C++, equivalent to `extends` in Java.

options: iostream should not be included in a header file
`using namespace std` should NOT be in a .h file — it pollutes the namespace of every file that includes this header
Class declarations are not allowed in .h files
Nothing is wrong
explanation: Putting `using namespace std` in a header file is bad practice because every file that includes the header inherits the namespace, which can cause naming conflicts. It should only be in .cpp files. The lab explicitly says 'shouldn't ever be included in your .h file!'

options: This is a parameterised constructor — it takes arguments
This is a default (no-argument) constructor that sets default values for a GearBin
This is a destructor
This is a copy constructor
explanation: A default constructor has no parameters. It initialises a GearBin with default values: size 'M', name 'Gear', max 25 parts. The `GearBin::` prefix is the scope resolution operator, indicating this implementation belongs to the GearBin class.

options: It runs when the object is created to set defaults
It runs when the object is destroyed/goes out of scope — used to free resources like heap memory
It prevents the object from being copied
It is the same as a default constructor
explanation: The destructor (prefixed with ~) is automatically called when an object is destroyed. It's used to release resources (like deleting heap-allocated memory). In this lab the destructors are empty because no heap memory is manually managed, but they are still declared as good practice.

options: A pointer dereference operator
The scope resolution operator — it tells the compiler that print() belongs to the InventoryBin class
A comparison operator
A namespace separator only
explanation: The `::` scope resolution operator specifies which class or namespace a function belongs to. In .cpp files, you must prefix method definitions with `ClassName::` so the compiler knows these implementations belong to that class.

options: private — so subclasses cannot access them
public — so anyone can access them
protected — so they are hidden from outside but still accessible in subclasses like GearBin
static — so they are shared across all instances
explanation: Protected members are accessible within the class and by derived (child) classes, but not by external code. Since GearBin and ValveBin inherit from InventoryBin and need to set partName and maxParts in their constructors, protected is the correct choice.

options: Overrides the parent print() without calling it — only prints gear size
Calls the parent class's print() method first, then adds GearBin-specific output
Causes infinite recursion
Compilation error — you cannot call parent methods in C++
explanation: `InventoryBin::print()` explicitly calls the parent class version of print(). This is how you invoke a parent method in C++ — using the scope resolution operator. GearBin extends the output by adding its gear-specific info after the parent output.

options: Yes — you must write `bin1 = new ValveBin()` in the constructor
No — declaring the variable in the class body automatically creates the object on the stack when BinManager is instantiated
You need to call bin1.create() manually
The objects are only created when first accessed
explanation: In C++, value-type member variables (not pointers) are created automatically when the containing object is created. The default constructor of ValveBin and GearBin runs automatically — no `new` keyword needed. This differs from Java where objects must always be explicitly constructed.

A do-while loop executes the body first, then checks the condition. First iteration: prints 5, x becomes 4. Check: 4 > 3 = true. Second iteration: prints 4, x becomes 3. Check: 3 > 3 = false. Loop ends. Output: '5 4'.

options: pBin is a regular InventoryBin object
pBin is a pointer to an InventoryBin — it stores a memory address and is initialised to null (no object yet)
pBin is a reference that copies the object
nullptr means the variable is static
explanation: In C++, `*` denotes a pointer — a variable holding a memory address. `nullptr` is a null pointer (points to nothing). `&bin1` takes the address of bin1, storing it in pBin. Pointers allow you to use a single variable to reference different objects (polymorphism via pointer).

options: A bitwise AND with bin1
The memory address of bin1 — assigns the location of bin1 to the pointer pBin
A reference copy of bin1
Declares bin1 as a reference variable
explanation: In C++, the `&` operator (when used on a variable, not in a declaration) is the address-of operator. `&bin1` gives the memory address of bin1. Assigning it to a pointer lets you use pBin to access bin1 via pointer operations.

options: A comparison operator — checks if pBin is greater than getNumParts
Member access through a pointer — equivalent to (*pBin).getNumParts()
A lambda arrow function
A return type annotation
explanation: `->` is the arrow operator for pointer member access. Since pBin is a pointer, you use `pBin->method()` instead of `pBin.method()`. It is shorthand for dereferencing the pointer and accessing the member: `(*pBin).method()`.

options: Creating a new ValveBin object
Casting the InventoryBin pointer to a ValveBin pointer — telling the compiler to treat pBin as pointing to a ValveBin
Checking if pBin is a ValveBin at runtime
Deleting the current pBin and replacing it
explanation: `static_cast` is C++'s compile-time cast. Casting an InventoryBin* to ValveBin* tells the compiler the object is actually a ValveBin. This is valid because bin1 is indeed a ValveBin. It is the C++ equivalent of Java's type.cast() — used to restore the concrete type from a base-class pointer.

question:
What does the `const` keyword do in this C++ method signature?

```cpp
int getNumBins() const;
```
options: The return value cannot be changed after being returned
The method promises not to modify any member variables of the object
The method can only be called on const objects
Both B and C are correct
explanation: A const member function (const after the parameter list) guarantees it won't modify the object's state. It also means you can call it on const objects. Both B and C are correct — const methods can be called on const objects, and they cannot modify member variables.

options: public — accessible everywhere
private — accessible only by the class itself and subclasses
protected — accessible by the class and its subclasses, but not by outside code
static — shared among all instances
explanation: Protected members are the 'middle ground': accessible within the class itself and by any derived (child) class, but not by unrelated external code. This is why partName and maxParts are protected — GearBin and ValveBin need to set them in their constructors.

options: #include <iostream>
#include <string>
#include <stdlib.h>
#import string
explanation: `string` in C++ is defined in the `<string>` header. Without it, the compiler doesn't know what `string` means. `<iostream>` provides cin/cout. Note: `using namespace std` should be in .cpp files only, but the include itself is fine in headers.

options: Only negative numbers
Two error conditions: negative part counts, and exceeding the bin's maximum capacity
Only overflow beyond maxParts
Null pointer dereference only
explanation: The code guards against two invalid states: (1) numParts being negative — you can't add a negative number of parts; (2) the total after adding exceeding maxParts — the bin has a physical capacity limit. success is only set to true when both checks pass.

options: print("Hello");
printf("Hello");
cout << "Hello" << endl;
Console.Write("Hello");
explanation: `cout << value << endl` outputs to standard output and adds a newline (endl). `printf` is C-style and works but isn't idiomatic C++. `print` doesn't exist as a standalone function. `Console.Write` is C#.

options: Nothing — both constructors do the same thing
It accepts arguments to set partQty and gearSize, whereas the default sets them to fixed starting values
It creates the object on the heap instead of the stack
Parameterised constructors are only called once per program
explanation: The parameterised constructor lets you customise the initial state — you pass in an int num for quantity and char size for gear size. The default constructor always starts with the same hard-coded values (e.g., gearSize = 'M'). This follows the same principle as Java constructor overloading.

options: Always calls InventoryBin::print()
Calls the appropriate subclass print() at runtime if print() is declared virtual
Always calls ValveBin::print()
Causes a compilation error
explanation: In C++, polymorphism via base-class pointers only works for `virtual` methods. If print() is declared `virtual` in InventoryBin, then pBin->print() calls the correct subclass version at runtime (ValveBin::print or GearBin::print). Without virtual, it calls InventoryBin::print regardless.

options: Option A
Option B
Option C
Option D
explanation: User-defined headers use double quotes: `#include "GearBin.h"`. Angle brackets `<>` are for standard library headers like `<iostream>` or `<string>`. `import` and `using` with a filename are not valid C++ include syntax.

options: valveType, partQty, maxParts
valveType, partName, maxParts
partName, valveType, partQty
gearSize, partName, maxParts
explanation: The lab specifies: default ValveBin sets valveType to 'Ball Valve', partName to 'Valve', and maxParts to 30. partQty is not set here (it's handled by the InventoryBin constructor). gearSize is a GearBin field, not ValveBin.

options: It returns the number of errors that occurred
It signals to the operating system that the program exited successfully (0 = success)
It clears all memory used by the program
It is optional and has no effect
explanation: By convention, `main()` returns an integer exit code to the OS. 0 means successful execution, non-zero values indicate errors. In modern C++ the compiler inserts `return 0` implicitly for main(), but it is good practice to include it explicitly.

options: They are identical in every way
C++ uses `: public ClassName` while Java uses `extends ClassName` — both achieve single inheritance of a parent class
C++ inheritance is always private by default
Java allows multiple inheritance of classes; C++ does not
explanation: Both achieve the same inheritance relationship with different syntax. C++ uses `: public BaseClass` (public inheritance means the parent's public/protected access is preserved). Java uses `extends`. Note: C++ actually supports multiple class inheritance, while Java does not (only interface multiple implementation).

options: Crashes with a segmentation fault
pBin remains nullptr, the null check prevents any operation, and success = false is returned
Automatically creates a new bin
Compilation error — switch must have a default case
explanation: If binNumber is 3, no case matches, pBin stays nullptr. The guard `if (pBin != nullptr)` prevents dereferencing a null pointer. The method returns false safely. This is defensive programming — always check pointers before using them in C++.

options: The maximum number of bins allowed
A counter tracking how many bins are managed — used to validate user input in the menu loop
The number of parts currently in all bins combined
A static count shared across all BinManager instances
explanation: numBins stores the count of managed bins (set to 2 in the constructor). It's used in main() to validate menu input: `while (choice < 0 || choice > binMgr.getNumBins())`. This prevents the user from selecting a bin number that doesn't exist.
